Metadata.defaultTitle: HSC Data — NSW ATAR Calculator & Scaling Data
Metadata.defaultDescription: Understand how the HSC and ATAR actually work. Free ATAR calculator, interactive scaling graphs, Band 6 honor roll, and data-driven insights. No myths — just mathematics sourced from NESA and UAC reports.
Metadata.ogDefaultTitle: HSC Data — NSW ATAR Calculator & Scaling Data
Metadata.ogDefaultDescription: Understand how the HSC and ATAR actually work. Free ATAR calculator, scaling graphs, honor roll, and data-driven insights.
Metadata.calculatorDescription: Estimate your ATAR from expected HSC marks using 2019–2025 UAC scaling data. Free, instant, no sign-up required. Compare courses and see your predicted rank.
Metadata.calculatorOgDescription: Estimate your ATAR from expected HSC marks using official UAC scaling data from 2019–2025.
Metadata.insightsTitle: Insights — How HSC Scaling & ATAR Work
Metadata.insightsDescription: The definitive guide to HSC scaling, moderation, and ATAR calculation. Debunking myths with data. Learn how your HSC marks are actually determined.
Metadata.insightsOgTitle: HSC Insights — How Scaling & ATAR Actually Work
Metadata.insightsOgDescription: The definitive guide to HSC scaling, moderation, and ATAR calculation. No myths — just the mathematical reality.
Metadata.scalingGraphsDescription: Visualise HSC scaling curves for every NSW course. Compare up to 8 subjects side by side. Understand exactly how raw marks translate to scaled marks.
Metadata.scalingGraphsOgTitle: HSC Scaling Graphs — Visualise Subject Scaling Curves
Metadata.scalingGraphsOgDescription: Interactive scaling graphs for every NSW HSC course. Compare subjects, see percentile curves, and understand how scaling works.
Metadata.honorRollDescription: Browse Band 6 and Distinguished Achiever results across NSW schools and courses. Filter by year (2001–2025) and subject. Free, searchable HSC data.

Metadata.honorRollOgDescription: Browse Band 6 and Distinguished Achiever results across NSW schools. Searchable data from 2001 to 2025.

Home.heroDescription: No myths. No rumours. Just the actual statistical mechanics of how NESA and UAC calculate your marks.
Home.moduleInsightsDesc: How HSC and ATAR actually work. Not myths — the mathematical reality.
Home.moduleCalculatorDesc: Estimate your ATAR from expected marks using 2025 UAC scaling data.
Home.moduleHonorRollDesc: Band 6 achievements across NSW schools. Filter by year and subject.
Home.ackP1: This project was built with respect for the teachers who made the HSC what it is — the ones who stayed back, wrote detailed feedback, and treated their students' results like they mattered. If you had one, you get it.
Home.ackP2: It's also for every student who cared enough to grind. The late nights, the early mornings — none of that disappears. If you earned a place on this list, it stays. If you're class of 2025 like me, maybe that pushes you to leave your name here too.
Home.ackP3: The HSC has no prerequisites. No connections, no background checks, no shortcuts — just turn up and do the work. It's a system that rewards effort, and that's worth acknowledging. This site compares you against thousands of others, but the only thing you control is yourself. Getting better than you were yesterday: that's the actual win.
Home.sourceNesaDesc: NSW Education Standards Authority — examination and moderation methodology
Home.sourceUacDesc: Universities Admissions Centre — scaling algorithms and ATAR calculation
Home.sourceSparoDesc: School Planning and Reporting Online — NSW public school annual reports
Home.sourcesDisclaimer: All data derived from official publications. Not affiliated with NESA, UAC, or any school.

Insights.heroDescription: Not what you have heard. Not what your tutor told you. What actually happens — derived directly from NESA and UAC technical documentation.
Insights.myth1Statement: Some subjects are 'scaled down' by the system
Insights.myth1p1: Scaling is not set by NESA or UAC — it is a mathematical outcome
Insights.myth1p2: A subject's scaled mean reflects the competitive strength of its candidature
Insights.myth1p3: If you rank first, you receive the highest scaled mark regardless of the subject
Insights.myth2Statement: My school's generous marking will boost my HSC
Insights.myth2p1: Schools submit only your rank, not your internal marks
Insights.myth2p2: Internal absolute marks are completely discarded during moderation
Insights.myth2p3: Your moderated mark depends entirely on your rank and your cohort's exam performance
Insights.myth3Statement: The bottom of my cohort will drag me down
Insights.myth3p1: Bottom students only anchor the minimum — your position is unaffected
Insights.myth3p2: Moderation uses three points: top exam mark, mean exam mark, bottom exam mark
Insights.myth3p3: Your moderated mark depends on your rank position, not on how weak the lowest student is
Insights.myth4Statement: This school or subject always scales badly — this year will too
Insights.myth4p1: Moderation is recalculated fresh every year using that cohort's actual exam results
Insights.myth4p2: No school-level historical data carries over into a new year's moderation
Insights.myth4p3: Last year's scaling report has zero predictive power for this year's outcome
Insights.myth5Statement: Being rank 2 with a big gap to #1 hurts the same as a small gap
Insights.myth5p1: The quadratic moderation curve preserves relative gaps between ranks
Insights.myth5p2: A 5% internal gap to rank 1 stays proportionally larger than a 1% gap after moderation
Insights.myth5p3: The tighter you close the gap, the closer your moderated mark to the person above you
Insights.myth6Statement: Only the top 10% of students get Band 6
Insights.myth6p1: Performance bands are standards-referenced — no fixed percentages, no quotas
Insights.myth6p2: Band cut-offs are decided each year by judging panels who review actual student responses against band descriptors
Insights.myth6p3: If every student meets the Band 6 standard, every student gets Band 6 — a course could have 50% B6 one year and 2% the next
Insights.pipelineSubheading: From the moment you sit your school assessment to the day you receive your ATAR.
Insights.pipelineNesaDomain: NESA Domain — What happens inside NESA
Insights.pipelineUacDomain: UAC Domain — What happens inside UAC
Insights.step01Plain: Your school submits a list of where everyone sits — first, second, third. That's it. The actual numbers are thrown away.
Insights.step01Takeaway: Both rank and internal marks matter — unless you are rank 1.
Insights.step01p1: Your school submits your relative position in each course
Insights.step01p2: Absolute internal marks (90 vs 95) are irrelevant — only the ranking matters
Insights.step01p3: If ranked #1, your moderated mark anchors to the highest exam mark in your cohort
Insights.step02Plain: This is your one totally fair moment — same paper, same conditions, same clock, against every other student in NSW.
Insights.step02p1: Your true performance under identical conditions across the entire state
Insights.step02p2: Raw marks are not reported to students
Insights.step02p3: Serves as the anchor for moderation and the input for scaling
Insights.step03Plain: Your school's internal marks don't survive. NESA uses your rank and your cohort's actual exam results to work backwards and assign you a fair mark.
Insights.step03p1: A quadratic curve maps your rank to a moderated mark using three anchor points
Insights.step03p2: The state-wide exam is the calibration — the fairest possible reference point because every school is measured against the same yardstick
Insights.step03p3: Rank order and relative gaps are preserved; school generosity is erased
Insights.step04Plain: Raw marks get translated into the familiar 0–100 band scale. No quotas. If everyone deserves a Band 6, everyone gets one.
Insights.step04p1: A judging panel reviews real student responses each year to decide the raw mark that meets Band 6 standard
Insights.step04p2: Band cutoffs are standards-referenced — no curve, no quota. If every student meets the descriptor, every student gets Band 6
Insights.step04p3: The aligned marks you see on your HSC report are NOT used by UAC for ATAR calculation
Insights.step05Plain: The number on your certificate — the one everyone talks about. But UAC glances at it and immediately ignores it.
Insights.step05Takeaway: The HSC mark on your certificate is for show. UAC is already looking past it.
Insights.step05p1: This is what NESA reports — the number you actually see
Insights.step05p2: Critical: UAC does NOT use this aligned mark for ATAR calculation
Insights.step05p3: UAC goes back to the raw marks. The aligned HSC mark is for reporting only
Insights.step06Plain: Behind the curtain, UAC takes the untouched raw marks — the ones you'll never see — and begins the actual ATAR calculation from scratch.
Insights.step06Takeaway: You will live your entire life not knowing the numbers that actually determined your ATAR.
Insights.step06p1: UAC receives your raw marks from NESA — before alignment to performance bands
Insights.step06p2: You will never see these values, yet they determine your entire ATAR
Insights.step06p3: The raw HSC mark is the mean of your raw exam and raw moderated assessment
Insights.step07Plain: Every subject gets placed on the exact same ruler — same average, same spread — so a 70 in Physics can be compared to a 70 in Drama.
Insights.step07p1: All courses are standardised to a common baseline: mean 25, SD 12 per unit
Insights.step07p2: This is a linear shift — it does NOT change your rank within the course
Insights.step07p3: Enables cross-subject comparison by putting all courses on the same scale
Insights.step08Plain: Scaling isn't set by a person. It's a mirror: a subject is 'strong' if the students who take it are strong across all their subjects. No one decides this — the data reveals it.
Insights.step08p1: A course's scaled mean = average scaled achievement of its students across ALL their subjects
Insights.step08p2: This creates a system of simultaneous equations solved iteratively until convergence
Insights.step08p3: A course's scaled mean rises when its students perform strongly in their other courses
Insights.step09Plain: Your total score. Best 10 units added up, with English guaranteed a seat at the table. Weak subjects simply don't count.
Insights.step09p1: Best 2 units of English + best 8 units from remaining courses (maximum 500)
Insights.step09p2: In 2025, 99.95 ATAR required an aggregate of 477.4
Insights.step09p3: Only the strongest subset of your results counts — weak subjects are discarded
Insights.step10Plain: This is not a mark. It is a percentile: what fraction of your entire age group you beat. ATAR 99.95 means only 0.05% of the state did better.
Insights.step10p1: ATAR is not a mark — it is a percentile rank against the entire HSC-aged population (ages 16-20)
Insights.step10p2: ATAR 99.95 means you outperformed 99.95% of the age cohort assuming they all sat the HSC
Insights.step10p3: In 2025, only 49 students achieved 99.95 across the entire state
Insights.step10p4: α = 1.5 − 2r is the spline's breakpoint — it's where the two polynomial halves meet and is determined entirely by the participation rate
Insights.step10p5: The median ATAR in 2025 was 70.75. Even the lowest ATAR (~30) already sits around the 45th percentile of the population — because everyone below simply didn't participate.
Insights.step10p6: A candidate at the 99th percentile among ATAR takers receives roughly ATAR 99.40. At the 98th: ~98.80. The spline compresses the top end, which is why 99.00 corresponds to ~top 1.7% of candidates.
Insights.step10p7: 1,045 students achieved ATAR ≥ 99.00 in 2025. That's 1.7% of ATAR recipients — not 1%. The more candidates (participation rose from 57k to 60k), the further this gap grows.
Insights.myth7Statement: ATAR 99 means I'm in the top 1%
Insights.myth7p1: ATAR ranks against the entire Year 12 age cohort — not just ATAR takers. About 43–45% of the age group doesn't sit the HSC or qualify for an ATAR.
Insights.myth7p2: In 2025, 60,432 students received an ATAR (participation rate ~56%). The bottom ~45% of the age population isn't even in the ranking — your percentile gets "pushed up" by their absence.
Insights.myth7p3: ATAR 99.00 ≈ top 1.7% of ATAR-eligible students. ATAR 90.00 ≈ top 17.3%. The gap between your ATAR number and your rank among candidates grows larger the further you go from 99.95.

Calculator.heading: ATAR Calculator
Calculator.description: Estimate your ATAR from expected HSC marks using 2019–2025 UAC scaling data.
Calculator.uacCalloutBefore: For the most accurate ATAR estimate, use the official
Calculator.uacCalloutAfter: . This calculator uses historical data and is a best-effort estimate. Results may differ.
Calculator.step1Full: Select your subjects: Choose your 10 or more units of study from the dropdowns below.
Calculator.step2Full: Enter your marks: Enter your estimated HSC marks (out of 100). For 1-unit extension courses, double your NESA mark (e.g. 45/50 → 90/100).
Calculator.step3Full: Analyse the result: The scaled marks and ATAR estimate based on past scaling data (2019–2025) are shown below.
Calculator.notation: a = insufficient scaling data, uses average of available years. Math Ext 1 = 2 units when paired with Ext 2. Math Advanced excluded when Ext 1 + 2 selected.
Calculator.disclaimerBefore: Uses UAC scaling data 2019–2025. Marks entered out of 100 for all courses (extension: double NESA mark). Results are estimates using historical statistics. For the most theoretically accurate ATAR estimate, use
Calculator.disclaimerAfter: — the official calculator. Not affiliated with NESA or UAC.
Calculator.warningMathAdvanced: Mathematics Advanced excluded: Extension 1 and Extension 2 take priority (max 4 calc units).
Calculator.warningNeed10Units: You need at least 10 units to be eligible for an ATAR.
Calculator.warningNeedEnglish: You need at least 2 units of English to be eligible for an ATAR.

ScalingGraphs.heading: Scaling Graphs
ScalingGraphs.description: Visualise how HSC marks translate to scaled marks. Select courses and compare scaling curves side-by-side.
ScalingGraphs.interpHsc1: Curve position: A higher curve means stronger scaling. The y-axis shows per-unit scaled contribution to your ATAR aggregate.
ScalingGraphs.interpHsc2: Curve steepness: A steeper slope means small HSC mark improvements translate to larger scaled mark gains.
ScalingGraphs.interpHsc3: Plateau at top: Some curves flatten at the top end — the scaled maximum is capped by the highest historical scaled score.
ScalingGraphs.interpPer1: Percentile interpretation: Beyond the 75th percentile, curves reflect the upper tail of the cohort — steep sections here mean top students in this subject are pulling away from the pack.
ScalingGraphs.interpPer2: Curve separation: The gap between curves at a given percentile shows how much more a top student in one subject contributes to ATAR versus another.
ScalingGraphs.interpYear: Year mode: "2025" uses the most recent data. "5-Year Avg" smooths out year-to-year fluctuations.

Changelog.heading: Changelog
Changelog.description: Recent updates and fixes to HSC Data.

items: Honor Roll page banner and subtitle simplified to show only the page title, now properly using i18n translations.
Refactored shared ATAR/scaling data layer into course-data.ts and spline.ts to eliminate duplicated code across modules.
Scaling graph comparison table now includes the maximum scaled mark (100 / #1 student) — previously only showed percentiles p50–p99.
Added Chinese (中文) localization — switch between English and Chinese via the header toggle. Translates all page content, UI labels, and SEO metadata (/zh/* routes).
SEO: dynamic metadata (title, description, Open Graph) now generated for every school and course detail page.
SEO: sitemap expanded to include all school detail pages, course detail pages, and changelog with hreflang alternates for bilingual indexing.
SEO: Open Graph social preview image added — dark-themed card shown when sharing links on social media.
SEO: BreadcrumbList structured data added to school and course detail pages for richer Google search results.
Fixed Band 1-6 incorrectly showing for Extension courses. Extension courses now correctly display E1-E4 band distribution for all years (2001-2025).
Added enrollment distribution and band performance charts to course detail views.
State rank mapping fixed — 2,200+ missing state ranks restored across school detail data.
Course name aliases expanded to 26 mappings covering all NESA course renames.
Legacy course codes (e.g. Mathematics → Mathematics Advanced) now merge into unified year-by-year data.
Honour Roll page rewritten with single-select year/school/course pickers and intersection views.
SPaRO school average data restored with rank badges and public school sourcing notes.
Added Compare page with bar charts, school maps, and category-based colour coding (currently hidden from nav).
Fixed enrollment distribution data — non_binary field was incorrectly storing total enrolment numbers.
Scaling graph comparison table now includes the maximum scaled mark (100 / #1 student) — previously only showed percentiles p50–p99.
Refactored shared ATAR/scaling data layer into course-data.ts and spline.ts to eliminate duplicated code across modules.
Honor Roll page banner and subtitle simplified to show only the page title, now properly using i18n translations.
